rules.title: Slovak Noun Cases - Overview
rules.text: Slovak has 7 grammatical cases that change noun endings based on their function in a sentence. Each case answers specific questions and is used in particular contexts. Nouns are also categorized by gender: masculine (M), feminine (F), and neuter (N).

rows: o kom? o čom? (about whom? about what?)
s kým? s čím? (with whom? with what?)

examples: Nominative: Muž je tu – The man is here
Genitive: Dom muža – The man's house
Dative: Dám mužovi knihu – I'll give the man a book
Accusative: Vidím muža – I see the man
Locative: Hovorím o mužovi – I'm talking about the man
Instrumental: Idem s mužom – I'm going with the man

rules.title: Complete Declension Table - All Cases
rules.text: Summary of noun endings across all cases, genders, and numbers. Note: (H) = hard consonant ending, (H-) = soft consonant ending, +vowel = add vowel, vowel→vowel = replace vowel.

rules.title: Masculine Nouns - Hard Consonants
rules.text: Masculine nouns ending in hard consonants (p, b, t, d, k, g, ch, h, m, n, r, l, s, z). Example: 'muž' (man).
examples: Nominative: Muž pracuje – The man works
Genitive: Dom muža – The man's house
Dative: Dám mužovi knihu – I'll give the man a book
Accusative: Vidím muža – I see the man
Locative: Hovorím o mužovi – I'm talking about the man
Instrumental: Idem s mužom – I'm going with the man

rules.title: Masculine Nouns - Soft Consonants
rules.text: Masculine nouns ending in soft consonants (j, c, dz, č, dž, š, ž, ť, ď, ň, ľ). Example: 'učiteľ' (teacher).
examples: Nominative: Učiteľ učí – The teacher teaches
Genitive: Kniha učiteľa – The teacher's book
Dative: Dám učiteľovi pero – I'll give the teacher a pen
Accusative: Vidím učiteľa – I see the teacher

rules.title: Masculine Inanimate Nouns
rules.text: Masculine inanimate nouns (objects, not people/animals) have different accusative forms. Example: 'stôl' (table).
examples: Nominative: Stôl je veľký – The table is big
Genitive: Noha stola – The table's leg
Accusative: Vidím stôl – I see the table (same as nominative for inanimates)
Locative: Kniha je na stole – The book is on the table

rules.title: Feminine Nouns - Type 'žena'
examples: Nominative: Žena pracuje – The woman works
Genitive: Kniha ženy – The woman's book
Dative: Dám žene kvetinu – I'll give the woman a flower
Accusative: Vidím ženu – I see the woman
Locative: Hovorím o žene – I'm talking about the woman
Instrumental: Idem so ženou – I'm going with the woman

rules.title: Feminine Nouns - Type 'ulica'
rules.text: Feminine nouns ending in -a with soft consonants before -a. Example: 'ulica' (street).
examples: Nominative: Ulica je dlhá – The street is long
Genitive: Koniec ulice – The end of the street
Locative: Bývam na ulici – I live on the street
Instrumental: Idem ulicou – I'm going along the street

rules.title: Feminine Nouns - Consonant Ending
rules.text: Feminine nouns ending in consonants (no -a). Example: 'kosť' (bone).
examples: Nominative: Kosť je tvrdá – The bone is hard
Genitive: Kúsok kosti – A piece of bone
Dative: Dám psovi kosť – I'll give the dog a bone
Accusative: Mám kosť – I have a bone

rules.title: Neuter Nouns - Type 'mesto'
examples: Nominative: Mesto je veľké – The city is big
Genitive: Centrum mesta – The city center
Dative: Idem k mestu – I'm going to the city
Accusative: Vidím mesto – I see the city
Locative: Bývam v meste – I live in the city
Instrumental: Som spokojný s mestom – I'm satisfied with the city

rules.title: Neuter Nouns - Type 'srdce'
examples: Nominative: Srdce bije – The heart beats
Genitive: Tlkot srdca – The heartbeat
Locative: V srdci – In the heart
Instrumental: So srdcom – With heart

rules.title: Nominative Case - Subject
rules.text: The nominative case is the basic dictionary form. It's used for the subject of a sentence (who/what performs the action).
examples: Muž pracuje – The man works
Žena číta – The woman reads
Dieťa spí – The child sleeps
Študenti sa učia – The students are learning

rules.title: Genitive Case - Possession and Negation
rules.text: The genitive case expresses possession (whose?), quantity, and is used after negated verbs. Also used with certain prepositions (bez, do, od, z, u, okolo).
examples: Dom otca – Father's house (possession)
Nemám psa – I don't have a dog (negation)
Kúsok chleba – A piece of bread (quantity)
Bez peňazí – Without money (preposition)
Od učiteľa – From the teacher

rules.title: Dative Case - Indirect Object
rules.text: The dative case indicates the indirect object (to whom/what something is given or done). Used with certain verbs and prepositions (k, ku, proti, vďaka).
examples: Dám mužovi knihu – I'll give the man a book
Pomôžem žene – I'll help the woman
Idem k doktorovi – I'm going to the doctor
Vďaka priateľovi – Thanks to a friend

rules.title: Accusative Case - Direct Object
rules.text: The accusative case marks the direct object (whom/what the action affects). Also used with certain prepositions (na, za, cez, pre, o). For masculine animate nouns, accusative = genitive. For masculine inanimate, accusative = nominative.
examples: Vidím muža – I see the man (animate = genitive form)
Vidím stôl – I see the table (inanimate = nominative form)
Čítam knihu – I'm reading a book
Idem na poštu – I'm going to the post office
Čakám na autobus – I'm waiting for the bus

rules.title: Locative Case - Location and Topic
rules.text: The locative case is always used with prepositions and indicates location (where?) or topic (about what?). Common prepositions: v/vo (in), na (on/at), o (about), po (after), pri (at/by).
examples: V meste – In the city
Na stole – On the table
O mužovi – About the man
Pri dome – At/by the house
Po obede – After lunch

rules.title: Instrumental Case - Means and Accompaniment
rules.text: The instrumental case expresses means (with what?), accompaniment (with whom?), and is used with certain verbs (byť = to be with professions). Common prepositions: s/so (with), medzi (between), pred (before), nad (above), pod (under), za (behind).
examples: Píšem perom – I write with a pen (means)
Idem s priateľom – I'm going with a friend (accompaniment)
Som učiteľom – I am a teacher (profession)
Medzi domami – Between houses
Pred školou – In front of the school

rules.title: Animate vs Inanimate Masculine
rules.text: Masculine nouns are divided into animate (people, animals) and inanimate (objects). This affects the accusative case: animate accusative = genitive form, inanimate accusative = nominative form.
examples: Animate: Volám otca – I'm calling father (otca = accusative)
Inanimate: Kupujem dom – I'm buying a house (dom = nominative form)

rules.title: Plural Nominative Patterns
rules.text: Plural nominative endings vary by gender and consonant type (hard vs soft).
examples: muž → muži (men)
žena → ženy (women)
mesto → mestá (cities)
učiteľ → učitelia (teachers)

rules.title: Genitive Plural - Vowel Removal
rules.text: Feminine and neuter genitive plural often involves removing the final vowel from the nominative singular and sometimes adding consonant changes.
examples: päť žien – five women
desať miest – ten cities
skupina mužov – a group of men
veľa ulíc – many streets
